html:
This article is about the Norwegian Crown Princess. For other Swedish royalty with the same name, see Margareta of Sweden.

Märtha of Sweden, born Princess Märtha Sophia Lovisa Dagmar Thyra of Sweden and Norway, of the House of Bernadotte, on March 28, 1901, in the parish of Hov, Stockholm, and died on April 5, 1954, at Rikshospitalet in Oslo, was a Swedish princess and Crown Princess of Norway, daughter of Prince Carl of Sweden and Princess Ingeborg. Märtha was a cousin to King Gustaf VI Adolf of Sweden. She was the sister of Queen Astrid of Belgium.

== Biography == Märtha became engaged on January 15th and married her cousin, Crown Prince Olav (later King Olav V of Norway), on March 21, 1929, in Oslo. The family lived at Skaugum, outside of Oslo.

=== Crown Princess of Norway === On January 26, 1940, shortly before Germany's occupation of Norway, she participated in the war effort by urging Norwegian women to mobilize for the country's defense. On April 9, 1940, the Crown Princess fled with her children from the Germans, first to Sweden, where she was considered to jeopardize Sweden's neutrality, and then to the United States, where she lived with her children for the remainder of World War II. Her husband and father-in-law, King Haakon VII of Norway, resided in England at the same time. She made a visit to London where she attended her father-in-law's birthday in 1942. During her stay in the United States, she became friends with President Roosevelt, and she is believed to have influenced his support for Norway. In 1942, she received a submarine chaser from the United States for Norway. Märtha's work on behalf of Norway made her very popular, and upon her return in 1945, she was welcomed as a heroine and given the name "the nation's mother." She died of cancer in 1954 after several years of illness.

== Legacy ==

=== Crown Princess Märtha's Church in Stockholm === The Norwegian church on Södermalm in Stockholm, named Kronprinsesse Märthas kirke (Crown Princess Märtha's Church), bears her name and was inaugurated in 1976. Outside the church stands a bronze statue of Crown Princess Märtha, created by the Norwegian sculptor Kirsten Kokkin. A corresponding statue is located outside the Norwegian residence in Washington D.C. and in Slottsparken (the park surrounding the Royal Palace) in Oslo.

=== Princess Märtha's Road in Stockholm === On Kungliga Djurgården in Stockholm, there is a road named after Crown Princess Märtha, called Prinsessan Märtas väg (Princess Märtha's Road), which is located near Villa Parkudden, where she spent her first seven summers. Outside Villa Parkudden, there is a plaque commemorating Crown Princess Märtha, unveiled on May 17, 2019. This date marked the 90th anniversary of when Crown Princess Märtha first stood on the balcony of the Royal Palace in Oslo to celebrate Norway's National Day. The plaque was created at the initiative of the Swedish-Norwegian Cooperation Fund.

=== M/S Kronprinsesse Märtha in Stockholm === There is a ship named after Crown Princess Märtha that was launched in Norway in 1929. Its original name was S/S Kronprinsesse Märtha, and it has changed names throughout its history. Since 2001, it has been a hotel ship under the name M/S Kronprinsesse Martha, located at Söder Mälarstrand in Stockholm.

=== Crown Princess Märtha's Area in Antarctica === This is an area within Queen Maud Land in East Antarctica called Kronprinsesse Märtha Kyst (Crown Princess Märtha Coast). The area was explored from the air by the Third Norwegian Expedition during the years 1929-1930 and was named after the Norwegian Crown Princess Märtha.

=== Fictional Appearances === In 2020, Sofia Helin portrayed Crown Princess Märtha in the Norwegian TV series Atlantic Crossing. In the film Kungens val (The King's Choice) from 2016, she was played by Tuva Novotny in a supporting role.

== Children == Ragnhild (1930–2012), married in 1953 to Erling Lorentzen (1923–2021), with one son and two daughters. Astrid (born 1932), married in 1961 to Johan Martin Ferner (1927–2015), with two sons and three daughters. Harald V of Norway (born 1937), married in 1968 to Sonja Haraldsen (born 1937), with one son and one daughter.

== Ancestors ==

== Sources ==

This article is wholly or partially based on material from the English-language Wikipedia page "Princess Märtha of Sweden," last modified March 22, 2010.

=== Notes ===

== Bibliography == There are five published books that explicitly deal with Princess Märtha. All were published in Norway and none have been translated into Swedish.

  • Märtha, Norges kronprinsesse 1929-1954: En minnebok (1995) [1]
  • Kronprinsesse Märtha: hustru, mor, medmenneske / Arvid Møller (1990) [2]
  • Dronningen vi ikke fikk: en biografi om kronprinsesse Märtha og kong Olav / Trond Noren Isaksen (2003) [3]
  • Reisen hun ikke ønsket / Einar Østgaard (2005) [4]
  • Kronprinsessens krig: den sanne historien om kronprinsesse Märtha og Franklin D. Roosevelt / Trond Norén Isaksen (2019) [5]

In the book "Norges tack: Svek, samverkan och solidaritet över Kölen 1940-1945," there is a section in Swedish about Princess Märtha.

== External links == The official website. Wikimedia Commons has media related to Märtha of Sweden.

How certain are we about this?

People from the 20th century are extremely well-documented. Uncertainty rarely concerns facts, but rather interpretation and evaluation, which can change over time and depending on the observer.
tldr: The text is about Princess Märtha of Sweden, who was the Crown Princess of Norway. She became a heroine during World War II and is now honored with her name on places, ships, and a church.
lattlast:
Princess Märtha was born in Sweden but married the Norwegian Crown Prince in 1929. During World War II, she fled to the United States with her children to protect them from the Germans. There, she worked tirelessly to secure aid for Norway and became very popular, earning her the title of "the nation's mother" upon her return.

Because of her importance to Norway, many things are named after her. These include a church in Stockholm, a road on Djurgården, and a ship located on the water in Stockholm. There is also an area in Antarctica, the southernmost continent, that bears her name.

Märtha died in 1954 after several years of illness. Her life has also been portrayed in various books and films, where she is depicted as a strong person who fought for her country during difficult times.
